title: Somatic Therapy for Eating Disorders: Reconnecting With Your Body
excerpt: Explore how somatic therapy helps heal eating disorders by fostering body awareness and nervous system regulation.
body:
Understanding Eating Disorders Beyond the Mind

Eating disorders are often treated as purely psychological conditions, focused on thoughts and behaviors around food. But anyone who has struggled with anorexia, bulimia, binge eating, or other disordered eating knows the experience runs far deeper than cognitive patterns. Your body holds the imprint of trauma, stress, and emotional pain that often fuels these disorders. Somatic therapy offers a powerful way to reconnect with your body, regulate your nervous system, and begin healing at a foundational level.

What Is Somatic Therapy?

Somatic therapy is a body-based approach to trauma and emotional healing. It emphasizes the role of the nervous system and bodily sensations in mental health. Unlike traditional talk therapy, somatic therapy engages the physical experience stored in your body to help release stuck trauma and restore regulation. As Bessel van der Kolk explained in The Body Keeps the Score, trauma is not just stored as memories but as physical tension and dysregulation in the body.

Somatic approaches draw on the work of pioneers like Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, and Stephen Porges, whose Polyvagal Theory provides a framework for understanding how our autonomic nervous system responds to stress and safety cues.

Why Somatic Therapy Is Crucial for Eating Disorders

Eating disorders are often a way the body and mind attempt to manage overwhelming emotions or unresolved trauma. Individuals with eating disorders frequently report feeling disconnected from their bodies or experiencing intense internal conflict about bodily sensations like hunger, fullness, or pain.

Somatic therapy helps by:

  • Restoring awareness of internal bodily sensations (interoception)
  • Calming and regulating the nervous system to reduce anxiety and overwhelm
  • Releasing trauma held in muscle tension, posture, and breath
  • Building a compassionate relationship with the body

Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, which integrates somatic and attachment theories, is especially relevant for eating disorders because it addresses how trauma and attachment wounds affect body awareness and regulation.

How Trauma and the Nervous System Impact Eating Disorders

Many people with eating disorders have histories of trauma, whether obvious or subtle. Trauma dysregulates the autonomic nervous system (ANS), often causing the body to remain in a state of hyperarousal (fight/flight) or hypoarousal (freeze/shutdown). This dysregulation can distort hunger and satiety signals, making it harder to trust the body.

“The body keeps the score: it remembers what the mind forgets.” — Bessel van der Kolk

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory explains how the vagus nerve mediates our sense of safety and connection. When the ventral vagal complex is engaged, we feel calm and able to connect. But trauma can trigger defensive states that disrupt this balance, leading to disconnection from bodily needs and sensations that eating disorders exploit.

Somatic Techniques That Support Healing

Somatic therapy uses a variety of practical tools to help individuals with eating disorders reconnect with their bodies safely and gradually. Here are some core methods:

1. Body Awareness and Mindfulness

Learning to notice physical sensations without judgment builds interoceptive awareness. This can start with simple practices like tracking breath, noticing tension areas, or feeling the weight of your body in a chair. Francine Shapiro’s EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing) also integrates body awareness to process trauma effectively.

2. Grounding and Resourcing

Deb Dana’s work on Polyvagal Theory emphasizes finding internal and external resources that promote a sense of safety. This could be feeling your feet on the ground, holding a comforting object, or visualizing a safe place. These techniques help regulate the nervous system and prevent overwhelm during sessions.

3. Movement and Breathwork

Gentle movement, such as sensorimotor exercises or yoga-informed practices, helps release tension and restore natural breath patterns. Peter Levine highlights how tremoring or shaking can be a natural physiological response to discharge trauma held in the body.

4. Tracking and Naming Sensations

Using language to identify what you feel physically—tightness, warmth, numbness—helps create a bridge between body and mind. Dan Siegel calls this integration of mind and body mindsight, which is key to self-regulation and healing.

Building a Compassionate Relationship With Your Body

One of the hardest parts of eating disorder recovery is learning to treat your body with kindness instead of criticism or punishment. Gabor Maté, who explores addiction and trauma, stresses that healing comes from compassion rather than shame.

Somatic therapy encourages you to listen to your body’s signals as messages instead of threats. This shift supports the restoration of trust—trust that your body knows what it needs and can guide you toward health.

How to Integrate Somatic Therapy Into Your Healing Journey

You don’t have to abandon traditional therapy; somatic therapy can be a powerful complement. Here are steps to get started:

  • Find a somatic therapist: Look for someone trained in Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, Somatic Experiencing, or Polyvagal-informed therapy.
  • Practice somatic exercises at home: Daily body scans, grounding, and gentle movement help you build awareness.
  • Journal your sensations and emotions: Track how your body responds to food, stress, and emotions.
  • Be patient: Reconnecting with your body takes time and consistent practice.

Conclusion: Reclaiming Your Body as Ally

Eating disorders can feel like a battle between mind and body, but somatic therapy shows us they are deeply intertwined. By addressing the nervous system and trauma stored in the body, you can start to break free from destructive patterns and reconnect with your body as a source of wisdom and safety.

This journey requires courage and compassion, but the science is clear: healing the body heals the mind. As Bessel van der Kolk sums it up, “To change the way we feel, we have to change the way we live in our bodies.”

faqs.q: How does somatic therapy differ from traditional talk therapy for eating disorders?
faqs.a: Somatic therapy focuses on bodily sensations and nervous system regulation, addressing trauma stored in the body, whereas talk therapy primarily targets thoughts and behaviors.

faqs.q: Can somatic therapy be used alongside other treatments for eating disorders?
faqs.a: Yes, somatic therapy complements traditional treatments like cognitive behavioral therapy and medical care by addressing physical and emotional regulation.

faqs.q: What if I feel disconnected or numb in my body during somatic work?
faqs.a: Somatic therapy is designed to be gradual and safe. Therapists help you build tolerance for sensations at your own pace, often starting with grounding and resourcing techniques.

faqs.q: How long does it typically take to see progress with somatic therapy?
faqs.a: Healing timelines vary, but many notice increased body awareness and reduced anxiety within weeks. Consistent practice and professional support are key for sustained change.

affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book that explains how trauma affects the body and offers insights into somatic healing.
